figures.gloss: The golden world-mountain at the centre of every world-system, ringed by seven golden ranges and seven seas, with the heavens stacked at its summit and the hells beneath its root — the axle around which the cosmos is built.
figures.facet: The same mountain under a new law: Buddhism inherited Meru entire from the Vedic world, then hollowed out its theism — Sumeru stands at the centre, but no creator sits on its peak, only Mara's heaven of desire and the impermanent gods of Indra's host.
figures.provenance: Systematised in the Abhidharmakośa (Vasubandhu, c. 4th–5th c. CE) and the Pali Abhidhamma; the geography is shared Indic inheritance from the older Meru cosmology.

figures.gloss: The lord of the desire-realm, death and delusion who assails the Buddha beneath the Bodhi tree with armies, temptresses and the demand 'by what right do you sit there?' — the one who would keep all beings bound to the wheel.
figures.facet: An adversary disarmed, not defeated: Māra is not slain like a chaos-dragon but simply seen-through — the Buddha touches the earth to witness him, and his armies dissolve. Evil here is a misreading of reality, not a rival kingdom.
figures.provenance: Pali Canon (Padhāna Sutta, Māra-saṃyutta) and later hagiography of the Bodhi-tree assault.

figures.gloss: Judge and king of the dead, who holds up the mirror of karma in which each soul sees its own deeds and is sent to its rebirth — inherited from the Vedic first-mortal and carried east into Chinese and Japanese hells as Yanluo and Enma.
figures.facet: A judge who only shows: Yama passes no arbitrary sentence — the karmic mirror reflects what the being itself has done, so judgement is exposure, not decree. The cosmic-order, not the king, condemns.
figures.provenance: Directly continuous with Vedic Yama; the East-Asian Ten Kings of Hell elaborate him through Tang-era apocrypha.

figures.gloss: The bodhisattva of boundless compassion, who hears every cry of the world and takes whatever form will save the one who calls — eleven heads, a thousand arms, and across the seas becoming Guanyin, Kannon, Chenrezig.
figures.facet: One mercy, ten thousand faces: where another tradition would split this into a pantheon, Buddhism insists the multiplicity is a single compassionate nature meeting each being in its own shape — the one-through-many made into a doctrine of skilful means.
figures.provenance: Lotus Sūtra (Universal Gateway chapter) and the Kāraṇḍavyūha; the gender shift to the female Guanyin occurs in Chinese transmission from the Tang onward.

figures.gloss: The doctrine that every Buddha is one awakened reality in three bodies — the formless truth-body (dharmakāya), the radiant heaven-body (saṃbhogakāya), and the historical man who walked and taught (nirmāṇakāya).
figures.facet: The unifying key of the whole shard: countless buddhas and bodhisattvas are not many gods but one awakened nature refracted — the trikāya is the explicit theology of the one-through-many, naming how the absolute appears as the manifold.
figures.provenance: Mahāyāna systematisation (Yogācāra; Laṅkāvatāra and Mahāyāna-sūtrālaṃkāra).

figures.gloss: The immense aeons of cosmic time, each world-system arising, enduring, dissolving in fire, water and wind, and lying void before it forms again — measured by similes of mountains worn away by a passing cloth once a century.
figures.facet: Destruction without a destroyer: the worlds dissolve and reform by impersonal law, not by a god's will or wrath — cyclic time as the breathing of dependent-origination itself, with no hand to start or stop it.

figures.gloss: The six-spoked wheel of the realms — gods, titans, humans, beasts, hungry ghosts, hells — turned by the three poisons at its hub and clutched in the jaws of Yama, the whole round of rebirth made into a single image.
figures.facet: A cosmos drawn as a diagnosis: the wheel is not a map to revere but a prison to escape, every realm including the heavens marked as impermanent suffering — the great-cycle reframed as the very thing salvation undoes.

figures.gloss: The teaching that there is no eternal self and no creator-ground — only a stream of conditioned arising, empty of any fixed essence, where the Vedic schools found the deathless Ātman one with Brahman.
figures.facet: The precious divergence — the shard that says NO: against the Upaniṣadic Brahman-Ātman and against every withdrawn-creator, Buddhism denies both the world-soul and the maker, putting impersonal law (dharma, dependent-origination) where other traditions put a god. Its 'cosmic-order' is order all the way down, with nothing behind it.
figures.provenance: Pali Canon (Anattalakkhaṇa Sutta) and Madhyamaka emptiness (Nāgārjuna). Stated here as an explicit, honoured contradiction of Brahman/Ātman — not a parallel.

figures.gloss: The Vedic thunder-king demoted to a Buddhist householder-god — lord of the Heaven of the Thirty-Three on Sumeru's summit, still wielding the thunderbolt, but now a pious if impermanent disciple subject to rebirth.
figures.facet: The sky-king as a cautionary tale: Śakra keeps Indra's vajra and his heaven, yet Buddhism makes him mortal and unenlightened — a once-supreme god enrolled as a student, the storm-wielder humbled beneath the law he too must obey.

figures.gloss: Siddhārtha Gautama of the Śākya clan, who attained enlightenment beneath the Bodhi tree at Bodh Gayā and spent forty-five years walking the Gangetic plain teaching the Four Noble Truths and the Eightfold Path before his final passing (parinirvāṇa) at Kuśinagara.
figures.facet: The roster's silent center: Māra who tempts him, Sumeru he sits beneath in cosmic terms, Yama who inherits his cosmology all orbit a founder who insisted he was not a god but a man who had simply seen clearly — the tradition's first move is to make its own founder unremarkable.
figures.provenance: Pali Canon biographical suttas (Ariyapariyesanā Sutta, Mahāparinibbāna Sutta) and later hagiographies (Buddhacarita, Lalitavistara); dates contested between the 'long' (c. 563–483 BCE) and 'short' (c. 480–400 BCE) chronologies.

figures.gloss: The bodhisattva presently residing in Tuṣita heaven, prophesied across Theravāda, Mahāyāna, and Vajrayāna sources to descend as the next Buddha once Śākyamuni's teaching has been entirely forgotten.
figures.facet: Buddhism's one true futurity: against a doctrine that mostly looks backward to the historical Buddha's teaching, Maitreya opens a horizon forward — one that repeatedly became a political weapon, fueling millenarian risings from the Chinese White Lotus movements to Korean and Japanese new religions.
figures.provenance: Attested from early Pali sources (Cakkavatti-Sīhanāda Sutta) and elaborated in Mahāyāna texts (Maitreyavyākaraṇa); millenarian mobilization documented across Chinese and Korean history.

figures.gloss: The Buddha of Infinite Light and Life, who vowed as the monk Dharmākara to build a Pure Land (Sukhāvatī) where any being who calls his name with sincere faith is reborn, freed from having to master the path alone.
figures.facet: Salvation reframed as invitation rather than achievement: where the roster's other figures walk or force the path, Amitābha's Pure Land answers a simple cry — the devotional shortcut that made him, for over a millennium, the most invoked name in East Asian religious life.
figures.provenance: The Larger and Smaller Sukhāvatīvyūha Sūtras; Pure Land schools formalized by Huiyuan (China, 4th c.) and Hōnen/Shinran (Japan, 12th–13th c.).

figures.gloss: The unconditioned cessation of craving, aversion, and delusion — the 'blowing out' of the fires that fuel rebirth — described almost entirely in negatives: not a place, not annihilation, not a continuation of self.
figures.facet: The stated answer left deliberately undefined: the roster maps every problem the wheel poses in exhaustive detail, but its solution is apophatic by design — nirvāṇa is what remains when the vocabulary of desire and rebirth simply stops applying.
figures.provenance: Pali Canon (Nibbāna Sutta, Udāna VIII); doctrinally elaborated across Abhidharma and Madhyamaka literature.

figures.gloss: Intentional action (karman, 'deed') as a self-regulating causal law: morally weighted acts of body, speech, and mind ripen into experience across lifetimes without any external judge administering reward or punishment.
figures.facet: The engine no one drives: Yama's mirror shows karma, it does not create it — the wheel turns on a law as impersonal as gravity, which is precisely how Buddhism dispenses with a creator-god and still explains a morally ordered cosmos.
figures.provenance: Pali Canon (Cūḷakammavibhaṅga Sutta); pan-Indic doctrine inherited and reworked from Vedic ritual causation into an ethical law of intention.

figures.gloss: The teaching that every phenomenon arises dependent on prior conditions in an unbroken twelve-linked chain — from ignorance through consciousness, craving, and grasping to birth and aging-and-death — with no first cause and no substance behind the chain.
figures.facet: The middle way stated as mechanism: dependent origination is how Buddhism avoids both eternalism (an unchanging self or God) and nihilism (nothing matters) at once — each link is real as process, none as essence, and the twelve links are literally what the Bhavacakra's rim depicts.
figures.provenance: Pali Canon (Paṭiccasamuppāda-vibhaṅga Sutta, Mahānidāna Sutta); systematized in the Abhidharmakośa and foundational to Nāgārjuna's Madhyamaka.

figures.gloss: The bodhisattva who vowed not to accept Buddhahood until the hells stand empty — a shaven-headed monk with a resonant staff and wish-granting jewel, working the depths beneath Sumeru's root to lighten the sentences of the damned and guard the newly dead, children, and travelers.
figures.facet: Compassion sent downward rather than out: where Avalokiteśvara answers every cry from above, Kṣitigarbha takes permanent residence in the one realm mercy is least expected to reach, completing the roster's vertical geography — heavens at the summit, hells at the root, a rescuer stationed at the bottom.
figures.provenance: Kṣitigarbha Bodhisattva Pūrvapraṇidhāna Sūtra; central to East Asian (especially Japanese Jizō) popular devotion documented from the Tang dynasty onward.

figures.gloss: The bodhisattva of transcendent wisdom (prajñā), an eternally youthful prince bearing a flaming sword that cuts through ignorance and a Perfection of Wisdom text resting on a lotus at his shoulder.
figures.facet: Wisdom given a body: Mañjuśrī personifies the very insight (śūnyatā, emptiness) the roster's Anātman entry states as doctrine — the Perfection of Wisdom literature's abstraction made into an approachable, youthful cult figure.
figures.provenance: Prajñāpāramitā literature and the Mañjuśrīmūlakalpa; central in Mahāyāna and Tibetan iconography as one of the Rigsum Gönpo (Three Protectors).

figures.gloss: The swift savioress, born in one telling from a lotus grown of Avalokiteśvara's tears of compassion, who appears in twenty-one forms — most widely Green Tārā the rescuer and White Tārā the healer — to deliver devotees from the eight great fears.
figures.facet: The one-through-many given a female face: like Avalokiteśvara's own multiplicity, Tārā's twenty-one forms are read as one compassionate nature parcelled into specific, invokable rescues — she became, across Tibet, Nepal, and the Himalayas, the most widely worshipped female figure in the Buddhist world.
figures.provenance: Tantric literature (Tārā-tantra, Sādhanamālā) and the Praises to the Twenty-One Tārās recited widely in Tibetan practice.

figures.gloss: The dome-shaped reliquary mound enshrining relics of the Buddha or later saints, circumambulated clockwise as an act of devotion — from the eight original stūpas raised over the Buddha's cremated remains to the thousands the emperor Aśoka is said to have built across South Asia.
figures.facet: The cosmic mountain rebuilt as monument: the stūpa's dome, spire, and honorific umbrellas echo Sumeru's own tiered heavens in miniature, turning the abstract world-mountain into a structure a lay devotee could walk around in an afternoon — the historical center of Buddhist lay practice.
figures.provenance: Vinaya accounts of the relic division after the Buddha's parinirvāṇa; Aśokan inscriptions and archaeology (Sāñcī, Bhārhut) attesting the stūpa cult from the 3rd century BCE.

figures.gloss: The monastic order of ordained monks (bhikkhu) and nuns (bhikkhunī) founded by the Buddha at Sārnāth, bound by the Vinaya's rules of discipline, tasked with preserving and transmitting the Dharma and serving as the field of merit for lay giving (dāna).
figures.facet: The institution the roster's doctrine assumes: Anātman and Dependent Origination are teachings that had to be carried by living bodies across two and a half millennia — the Saṅgha is the third of the Three Jewels precisely because a philosophy this demanding does not transmit itself.
figures.provenance: Vinaya Piṭaka; the Buddha's founding of the order is recounted in the Mahāvagga.

figures.gloss: Serpent water-beings of rivers, lakes, and ocean depths, alternately fearsome and protective — Mucalinda, the nāga-king, coils his hood over the newly awakened Buddha to shield him from a week-long storm, and nāgas are said to have guarded the Perfection of Wisdom sūtras undersea until Nāgārjuna could retrieve them.
figures.facet: The tradition's chief hinge to local cult: wherever Buddhism travelled, it met pre-existing serpent-spirit veneration and absorbed rather than displaced it — nāgas remain the standing point of contact between the Dharma's high philosophy and the water-spirit cults of Southeast Asia, Tibet, and beyond.
figures.provenance: Vinaya account of Mucalinda; the Nāgārjuna legend recorded in Chinese pilgrim accounts (Xuanzang) and Tibetan historiography.

figures.gloss: The 8th-century tantric master credited with subduing Tibet's local demons and spirits and binding them by oath to protect the Dharma, thereby establishing Vajrayāna Buddhism in Tibet and founding Samye, its first monastery.
figures.facet: The culture-hero's conquest turned cosmological housekeeping: Padmasambhava's feat is not stealing fire but subjugating an entire indigenous spirit-world, converting hostile local deities into sworn dharma-protectors (dharmapāla) rather than expelling them.
figures.provenance: Tibetan hagiography (the Padma bka'-thang and later termas); historically attested arrival of Indian tantric Buddhism in Tibet under King Trisong Detsen, 8th century CE.

figures.gloss: The bodhisattva of spiritual power, wrathful wielder of the vajra (thunderbolt/diamond-scepter) and the third of the Rigsum Gönpo, the 'Three Protectors,' alongside Mañjuśrī's wisdom and Avalokiteśvara's compassion.
figures.facet: Indra's thunderbolt reissued: Vajrapāṇi's weapon is iconographically descended from Śakra-Indra's own vajra, redeployed not to rule a heaven but to guard the Dharma by force where wisdom and compassion alone cannot compel.
figures.provenance: Pali and Mahāyāna sources on the yakṣa-turned-bodhisattva Vajrapāṇi; iconographic descent from Indra's vajra traced by scholars including Étienne Lamotte.

figures.gloss: The Buddha's first sermon at Sārnāth, framed as a physician's diagnosis: that existence is suffering (dukkha), that suffering has a cause (craving), that the cause can cease, and that an Eightfold Path of right view, conduct, and meditation leads to that cessation.
figures.facet: The tradition's own summary of itself: the wheel to escape, the karma that turns it, the nirvāṇa that ends it — everything else in this roster is already contained in this one fourfold diagnosis, delivered as clinical method rather than commandment.
figures.provenance: Pali Canon, Dhammacakkappavattana Sutta ('Setting the Wheel of Dharma in Motion').

edges.note: Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both are ubiquitous, compassionate figures who transcend formal hierarchies to constantly rescue and guide the faithful in distress.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A threefold manifestation of ultimate reality, expressing the absolute, its energetic transmission, and its physical realization on earth.

Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Where Buddhism systematically dismantles the self into transient aggregates (anatman), Anthroposophy positions the eternal 'I' as the indestructible spiritual crown of the lower sheaths.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both represent the ultimate, absolute adversary not just to moral goodness, but to the very core of individual spiritual awakening and the human 'I'.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The supreme vow of sacrifice: a cosmic sovereign who refuses to move onward in his own immense spiritual evolution in order to rescue a failing, darkened world.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Cosmic figures of illusion whose primary function is to block liberation and maintain cyclical bondage.
GOLD SEAM: The Manifestation functions similarly to the Nirmanakaya, bridging the absolute truth (Dharmakaya) to the temporal world.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Shares the exact same Indian cosmological roots of the central world-mountain, adapted into stone architecture.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Buddhist awakening relies on realizing the self does not fundamentally exist; BHI awakening relies on realizing the self is an eternal, genealogical absolute.

FLAG — rivalry/borrowing: Bön's enlightened first-teacher is modelled with the same three-body/enlightened-buddha structure as Mahāyāna, set up as the PRIOR original. Conspicuous mutual modelling (Snellgrove, Kvaerne), but as a structural parallel of organised systems it is tiered 3, not a proven derivation.

FLAG: like the Buddha beneath the Bodhi tree, Tönpa Shenrab subdues the demon-host and binds the tempter-spirits to oath — the awakener-versus-adversary scene shared with (and plausibly patterned on) the Māra episode.

FLAG: the developed Yungdrung-Gutsek cosmology shares the central-axis-mountain structure with Buddhist Sumeru within the same Tibetan literate milieu — documented coexistence and mutual shaping of the two cosmologies; the swastika-tiering remains Bön's own signature.

FLAG: Satrig Ersang as the supreme wisdom-mother strongly converges with the Mahāyāna Prajñāpāramitā 'Mother of the Buddhas'; her developed theology is post-Buddhist within the shared Tibetan idiom — a documented kinship of the wisdom-matrix, not a coincidence.

FLAG: the later (Buddhist-shaped) bardo passage runs the dead toward judgement and rebirth under the Yama/karmic-mirror schema of Tibetan Buddhism — a documented borrowing into the later death-stratum, distinct from the archaic tomb-cult.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The Arahant is the living, breathing culmination of the Anatman principle, having experientially extinguished the illusion of an independent self.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Avalokitesvara is the ultimate mythological personification of the abstract Bodhisattva ideal, embodying the active compassion that delays Nirvana.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The Dharmakaya (Truth Body) of the Trikaya doctrine is explicitly identified with Sunyata—the empty, unconditioned nature of all Buddhas.
Sumeru IS Meru — the same world-mountain, the same Sanskrit name (su-meru, 'excellent Meru'), carried from Vedic into Buddhist cosmology as direct Indic inheritance. The brightest seam in the shard.
The quartered cosmos around a central mountain — joint membership, shared with Yggdrasil, the Ceiba, Eden's rivers and Meru itself.
One and the same deity by descent: Vedic Yama, first-mortal and king of the dead, inherited wholesale into Buddhism and carried east as Yanluo / Enma. Indic-internal cognate.
Śakra is Indra — the Vedic storm-king himself, name and vajra retained, but reduced to an impermanent heaven-god subject to rebirth. Direct inheritance, then demotion.

The load-bearing divergence: anātman is the explicit DENIAL of the Brahman/Ātman of the Upaniṣads — same Indic conversation, opposite verdict. No eternal self, no world-soul. Tiered 1 because the contradiction is a documented historical argument within one tradition, not a loose parallel.
Against the Sāṃkhya eternal Puruṣa (the witness-self), Buddhism denies any abiding witness — a documented doctrinal dispute between Indian schools.

Within the shard Māra and Yama are twin faces of death — Māra-as-death the tempter who keeps beings bound, Yama-as-death the judge who sorts them; in some texts Māra is even called the lord over the realms Yama rules.
Māra (or Yama) clasps the Wheel of Becoming in his jaws — the adversary IS the round of rebirth that the Buddha steps out of.
Avalokiteśvara's thousand forms are the trikāya doctrine in practice — one awakened nature taking every shape; the principle and its richest example, both Mahāyāna.
Two scales of the one cyclic vision — the kalpas the great breathing of world-systems, the wheel the turning of individual rebirth; both impersonal, both within the shard.

Speculative: the Bodhi-tree temptation rhymes with the wilderness temptation of Christ by Satan — the tempter who tests the awakener with power and doubt. A structural rhyme of motif, no contact; flagged.
The temptation-and-victory pattern: a saviour assailed by the prince of this world and overcoming through refusal — Bodhi tree and wilderness as the same narrative joint, independently arrived at.
Judge-of-the-dead who weighs deeds: Yama's karmic mirror and Anubis's scale both make the verdict an exposure of what the soul itself carries — order judging, not whim.
Sovereign of the land of the dead through which all the unliberated must pass — a structural parallel of the underworld-king, no contact.
King and judge of the dead presiding over the reckoning — analogous office, separate families.
Two psychopomps within the shard at opposite poles — Avalokiteśvara the mercy that guides souls out of the hells, Yama the law that assigns them; compassion and judgement on the same wheel.
The compassionate intercessor who hears the suffering and saves — Guanyin and Isis as the merciful divine mother answering the cry; functional parallel across families.

Both name the one reality appearing as the many — yet they diverge at the root: Brahman is the eternal Self the trikāya's dharmakāya pointedly is NOT. Kinship in structure, documented opposition in content.
One sacred reality refracted into countless masks (ixiptla / nirmāṇakāya) — the one-through-many reached independently in Mesoamerica and in Mahāyāna.
The formless absolute (dharmakāya / Ein Sof) that emanates the manifest worlds — a structural parallel of the unmanifest ground, no contact.
Impersonal cosmic process unfolding by its own law without a willing creator — the kalpas and the Dao both put order in the place where other traditions put a god.
Both name an order with no personal maker behind it — cosmic-order as the floor of reality; a true functional convergence, distinct families.
Time as successive worlds destroyed and remade — the Mesoamerican Five Suns and the Buddhist kalpas both read history as recurring catastrophe-and-renewal, independently.

The same god, inverted in rank: Vedic Indra is the supreme storm-king; Buddhist Śakra is the same figure demoted to an impermanent, unenlightened disciple — a documented theological re-ranking within Indic religion.
The thunderbolt-wielding sky-king atop the world-mountain's heaven — Śakra and Zeus as the storm-sovereign archetype, with Buddhism uniquely subordinating him to a higher law.
Speculative: the lord of delusion and the smoking-mirror lord of fate and temptation both rule the realm of binding desire — a reach of theme, no descent; flagged.
The world-axis with heavens above and hells below — Sumeru and the world-tree as the same cosmological spine, separate families.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: In the syncretism of Tibetan Buddhism and Buryat shamanism, the chief of the 33 Buddhist heavens was mapped directly onto the chief of the 55 western Tengeri.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The historical merging of the indigenous shadow-lord with the karmic judge of the dead imported by Tibetan lamas.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The Mother's cult is heavily syncretized with Quan Am (Avalokitesvara), sharing her role of ultimate maternal compassion.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The Caodaist theological timeline fundamentally operates on adapted Buddhist kalpas (cosmic cycles) to justify its Amnesties.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both represent the psychological force of illusion that uses fear and physical distraction to keep the mind trapped in the dream of matter.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Chaos magic temporarily weaponizes the state of no-self (anatman) through intense trance, dissolving the ego to allow raw intent to pass unobstructed.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both represent the psychological forces of illusion and distraction that actively fight to keep the practitioner trapped in consensus reality.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Instead of dissolving the self into emptiness, Cheondoism elevates the human self to the absolute ontological status of Heaven.
GOLD SEAM: Mazu is widely syncretized with Guanyin (Avalokitesvara) in coastal regions, viewed either as her incarnation or a complementary goddess of compassion.
GOLD SEAM: The City God's judicial courts in the underworld heavily absorbed the iconography and bureaucratic torture chambers of the Buddhist Yama.
GOLD SEAM: The supreme tempter who confronts the savior in the wilderness, offering worldly dominion to distract them from their world-saving mission.
GOLD SEAM: A structural resonance where ultimate reality manifests in three distinct but inseparable dimensions (Father/Dharmakaya, Son/Nirmanakaya, Spirit/Sambhogakaya).
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both concepts require the complete dissolution of the isolated ego to achieve true comprehension of reality.
GOLD SEAM: The supreme embodiment of infinite compassion and humaneness toward the suffering of others, anchoring salvation in empathy.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both envision a universe functioning on inexorable, impersonal mechanistic laws without the need for active divine micromanagement.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both feature a mechanically turning wheel of existence, often gripped by a demonic or blind force, dragging souls through cyclic states.

One protective nature meeting each community in a local form — the Amman multiplied across villages and Avalokiteśvara taking whatever shape will save: the one-through-many lived as locality on the Tamil side, as skilful-means on the Buddhist.
GOLD SEAM: A continuous cycle of rebirth that traps the soul in the material world, though the Druze version strictly lacks species-fluidity and progressive karma.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both represent a structural 'negative power' that uses the illusions of mind, desire, and reincarnation to bind souls to the lower realms.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both are supreme embodiments of spiritual compassion who vow to remain active in the lower worlds to guide souls to liberation.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The absolute nothingness of death in Epicureanism invalidates the existence of the moral judge of the dead and the wheel of rebirth.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both are highly structured, numbered doctrines designed practically as psychological medicine to cease human suffering.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both embody the forces of spiritual ignorance and attachment that keep beings trapped in cycles of suffering rather than elevating to light.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A supremely compassionate being dedicated entirely to the salvation and deliverance of all sentient beings from suffering.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Falun directly reappropriates the traditional Buddhist Dharma wheel, re-engineering it into an implantable cosmic mechanism.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A direct, literal adaptation of the Mahayana Dharmakaya/Nirmanakaya concept, operationalized as the Master's ubiquitous protective emanations.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both represent the soporific, omnipresent force that keeps humanity entranced in cyclical, reactive illusion and prevents objective awakening.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both fiercely assert that the default human condition possesses no permanent, unchanging self or 'I', but is merely an aggregate of shifting, temporary reactions.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Conscious labor is positioned as the exact, excruciating effort required to cease turning blindly on the mechanical wheel of existence.
GOLD SEAM: The central, unshakeable axis around which the practitioner constructs their interior spiritual geography.
Mani named the Buddha among the prior messengers of Light; Manichaeism spread east in explicitly Buddhist dress along the Silk Road. The bridge-tradition's deliberate reach toward a fourth shard.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both represent the forces of illusion and temptation that the spiritual operator must master and bind.
GOLD SEAM: Elaborate pictorial wheels of cyclic ages, states of being, and cosmic law turned by the initiate through meditation.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Where Sumeru is the celestial mountain elevating the cosmos, Echeyde is the infernal pillar threatening to erupt and consume it.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both represent the absolute cosmic axis around which all celestial bodies logically rotate.

GOLD SEAM: Both are cosmic mountains or spheres that the soul must ascend — the seven spheres are the planetary heavens, while Mount Sumeru is the axis of the Buddhist cosmos. Both represent the vertical structure of the universe.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The bureaucratic sovereign of the afterlife who maintains the ledgers of lifespans and dispatches messengers to collect the dying.
GOLD SEAM: Both systems locate ultimate liberation in the extinguishing of the illusion of the independent ego, though Sufism subsumes it into the Divine presence rather than a void.
GOLD SEAM: The architectural and spiritual center of the world system, operating as the cosmic navel around which believers perform ritual circumambulation.

Sibling Indic systematisations of beginningless cyclic time — both teach worlds rising and falling by impersonal law with no creator to start the round; sister-śramaṇa traditions of the same Gangetic ferment.
Two śramaṇa answers from the same age and region: both displace Vedic blood-sacrifice and a creator-god, Jainism by the vow of non-harm, Buddhism by no-self — kindred refusals, not derivations.
The sharpest fault-line between the śramaṇa siblings: Jainism makes the eternal plural soul the bedrock of reality; Buddhism denies any abiding self at all. Same problem, opposite verdict.
Both śramaṇa cosmographies inherit the Meru-centred vertical world and then hollow out its theism — a makerless axis-mundi in two neighbouring traditions.

The same śramaṇa victory under one image: Mahāvīra the 'victor' (jina) conquers all passion by asceticism just as the Buddha sees through Māra's armies — contemporaries of one age and region, kindred conquests of desire, not one borrowed from the other.
Both deny a creator-ground, yet the freed Jain jīva remains an eternal individual where the Buddhist nirvāṇa extinguishes the very notion of an abiding self — kindred denials, opposite destinies.

GOLD SEAM: Both are tempters who oppose spiritual progress — Ha-Satan tests human righteousness, while Mara tempts the Buddha with desire and fear. Both are forces of illusion and attachment, not ultimate enemies of God.

Both address the problem of desire and the self — but the yetzer hara is a God-given impulse to be channeled, while anatman is the doctrine of no-self to be realized. A loose parallel of the human condition, flagged.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A radical philosophical inversion; where Buddhism seeks the realization of no-self (Anatman), Luciferianism demands the hardening and eternalization of the individual ego.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The central, immense cosmic mountain around which the spiritual and geographical world is organized.
GOLD SEAM: Central Asian Uighur Manichaeans equated Az, the demon of desire, directly with Mara, utilizing Buddhist asceticism to explain the imprisonment of light.
GOLD SEAM: Both operate as the cosmic spine connecting the realms of suffering to liberation, reinterpreted by Manichaeans as a literal machine for soul-transport.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A meticulously constructed architectural center that serves as the inescapable pivot of the cosmic and political order.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: While both use the term kalpa to denote cosmic time, the Brahma Kumaris compress this into a hyper-fatalistic, infinitely repeating cycle of exactly 5,000 years.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Later Mongolian Buddhism explicitly identified Qormusta with Indra (Sakra), the lord of the thirty-three heavens.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Heavily syncretized with Yama, the Dharmaraja, becoming the terrifying judge of karmic deeds in Mongolian Buddhism.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Thagyamin is a direct localized adoption of the Buddhist Sakra, serving as the moral governor of lower spirits.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Where Mara is the Buddhist demon of temptation to be defeated, Ko Gyi Kyaw is the spirit of temptation to be eagerly embraced and propitiated.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A vast, orchestrated illusion designed specifically to keep divine beings trapped in a state of ignorance and slumber.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both are infinitely compassionate, conscious entities that actively manifest in the physical world to guide beings out of ignorance and suffering.
GOLD SEAM: While Buddhism liberates by extinguishing the illusion of self, the New Age liberates by infinitely expanding the self until it encompasses the divine.
GOLD SEAM: Functions identically to the bodhisattva vow: delaying total cosmic merger in order to continuously intercede for suffering humanity.
GOLD SEAM: While Buddhism liberates by extinguishing the illusion of a permanent self, New Thought liberates by expanding the self until it is utterly equated with the Divine.
GOLD SEAM: Both function as the chief adversary to enlightenment not through brute force, but through the weaponization of illusion and false belief.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both measure cosmic time in vast, structured epochs, but Aeonics seeks to forcefully abort the epoch rather than escape it.
GOLD SEAM: The profound diagnosis of existence as an agonizing cycle of repeated births that the ascetic must break to achieve true liberation.
GOLD SEAM: While Buddhism denies the eternal soul and Orphism centers it, both radically agree that the material self is a trap generating endless suffering.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Spinoza's realization that the individual self is merely a transient mode aligns with the Buddhist dissolution of the ego into the interdependent whole.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The spirit house functions as a localized, domestic micro-axis mundi, mimicking the cosmic mountain on a mundane scale.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Phaya Thaen is routinely identified and syncretized with the Buddhist Indra as the sovereign of the heavens.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: While Mara tempts from without, the Phi Pop consumes from within; both represent the corruption of spiritual discipline into destructive craving.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The cyclical trap of repeated births where moral impurity guarantees continued material imprisonment.
GOLD SEAM: A pervasive, illusion-generating force that binds humanity to a corrupt material cycle, which must be overcome through enlightenment.
GOLD SEAM: While Buddhism dissolves the self into non-self to end suffering, Rastafari elevates the self into the absolute 'I' to conquer oppression.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The central cosmic mountain that serves as the unshakeable geographic and spiritual axis of the world.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both focus on the liberating realization that the permanent, damnable 'soul' imposed by societal conditioning does not actually exist.
GOLD SEAM: LaVeyan I-theism centers entirely on the supreme reality of the individual ego, completely opposing the Buddhist dissolution of the self.
GOLD SEAM: Mara represents the illusions of earthly desire that must be defeated; Satanism embraces these exact desires as the only true, vital reality.
GOLD SEAM: Buddhism liberates by extinguishing the illusion of a permanent self; Scientology liberates by recovering the absolute permanence and causal power of the self.
GOLD SEAM: Both act as the chief adversary to enlightenment through the weaponization of illusion, craving, and automated stimulus-response, rather than through positive malice.
GOLD SEAM: The continuous cycle of stimulus and response dictated by engrams mirrors the mechanical, repeating nature of samsara driven by accrued karma.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The fundamental realization that there is no eternal, unchanging self or soul that survives the dissolution of the body.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both represent the inexorable, turning cycle of existence and the mechanism of overcoming it, though Shaivism personifies it as a kinetic dancer.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The ultimate reduction of the ego and the physical form to nothingness, revealing the total illusion of permanent personal identity.
GOLD SEAM: The supreme adversary is not a literal demon, but the internal illusion of a separate self that keeps the soul trapped in the cycle of transmigration.
GOLD SEAM: Spiritualism is entirely predicated on a permanent, continuously evolving individual ego, directly opposing the Buddhist doctrine of non-self.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Figures who have advanced spiritually but deliberately remain accessible on the same plane to assist others in their own spiritual awakening.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both conceptualize the supreme divine principle transitioning into a comprehensible human form (Nirmanakaya / Divine Human) without dividing its unified cosmic essence.

Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: While Buddhism dissolves the core self into non-self, Swedenborgianism crystallizes it, asserting that a singular, eternal 'ruling love' permanently defines personal identity.
GOLD SEAM: In Chinese popular religion and medieval syncretism, the Jade Emperor was frequently equated with Indra as the ruler of the thirty-three heavens.
GOLD SEAM: The Taoist triad of the Three Pure Ones structurally adapted elements of the Buddhist Trikaya (Three Bodies of Buddha) during medieval sectarian competition.
GOLD SEAM: Where Buddhism seeks liberation through the realization of no-self (anatman), Neidan seeks to condense and forge an indestructible, eternal self.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both represent the forces of illusion and entrapment that keep human consciousness asleep and bound to mindless, cyclic existence.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A radical philosophical inversion; where Buddhism seeks the realization of no-self (Anatman), Setianism seeks the absolute hardening and apotheosis of the individual ego.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A supremely compassionate maternal figure dedicated entirely to the ongoing salvation, healing, and deliverance of all human beings.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The radical deconstruction of the individual self, insisting that the physical body is not one's own permanent possession.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Radically opposed spiritual goals; Buddhism seeks the dissolution of the self (Anatman), whereas Left-Hand Path apotheosis seeks the eternal preservation of the ego.
GOLD SEAM: Both are personifications of egoic illusion and dispersion that attempt to prevent the seeker from crossing over to liberation.
GOLD SEAM: Both map existence onto unimaginably vast, cyclic timeframes where universes and humanities sequentially emerge and dissolve.
GOLD SEAM: The Mahatmas echo the Bodhisattva ideal, delaying their own ultimate liberation to assist the spiritual evolution of humanity.
GOLD SEAM: Shambhala, the etheric seat of Sanat Kumara, functions as the cosmic center and spiritual axis of the world, echoing Mount Meru.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both are seen as compassionate figures working to alleviate global suffering by distributing a path to higher consciousness.

Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: They are diametric opposites. Buddhism seeks liberation by realizing the self is an illusion to be dissolved; Transhumanism seeks liberation by meticulously mapping and preserving the self forever.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both figure death as an adversarial force that keeps sentient beings trapped and suffering, demanding heroic intervention to overcome its dominion.
GOLD SEAM: While both advocate detachment from the physical body, Buddhism denies the eternal self, whereas Heaven's Gate asserts an eternal alien consciousness occupying the vehicle.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The embodiment of absolute compassion—highly evolved spiritual beings who defer their own ultimate rest to endlessly counsel and heal suffering humanity.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The absolute refusal to accept a final cosmic resolution while any being remains in a state of suffering, damnation, or exile.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The utilization of unimaginably vast scales of time to account for the slow, required evolution of human spiritual perfection.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The concept of a supreme cosmic entity manifesting a specific, localized physical emanation body to physically interface with finite creatures.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The manifestation of the absolute truth into a localized, phenomenal body (Nirmanakaya) for the specific soteriological benefit of trapped beings.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The soteriological pivot away from grueling ascetic self-effort and toward pure, emotional surrender to an entity of infinite compassion.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: While Buddhism denies a persistent self across rebirths, Dunadun insists on a singular, accountable essence moving through different physical garments.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The unshakeable cosmic center where the divine intersects with the earthly, though Perdiwar is anchored to a literal river bridge rather than a mythological peak.
GOLD SEAM: The central cosmic mountain or peak that acts as the geographic and spiritual anchor of the physical world.
GOLD SEAM: A continuous cycle of rebirth that the soul must navigate until it achieves sufficient purification.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Directly contradicts the orthodox Buddhist doctrine of no-self by asserting an eternal, primordial soul that must be saved.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Borrows the Buddhist concept of cyclical eons but compresses them into a finite, urgent three-stage countdown.

GOLD SEAM: the wheel of becoming turns on karma — deeds steering rebirth — while niyati denies deeds any purchase at all; the Pali canon rates Makkhali's fatalism the most pernicious of doctrines, so the opposition itself is attested.
GOLD SEAM: sibling śramaṇa arithmetics of vast cosmic time — the Ajivika count of 8,400,000 great aeons survives embedded in the Buddhist report of the rival doctrine.
GOLD SEAM: The three-age scheme and the coming assembly descend directly from Buddhist kalpa-cosmology and the canonical Maitreya prophecy, compressed to an imminent horizon.
Kinpusen tradition reads Zao Gongen as the composite manifestation of Shakyamuni, Kannon (Avalokitesvara), and Maitreya — the bodhisattva of compassion folded into a mountain god's wrathful body.
The Principal Book identifies Il-Won-Sang explicitly with the Dharmakaya Buddha — the Truth Body of Trikaya doctrine drawn as a single circle.
World-ages destroyed and remade in succession, catastrophe read as the basic rhythm of cosmic time rather than an aberration.

FLAG: the tempter-demon converted rather than simply destroyed occupies the same narrative slot as Māra opposing Śākyamuni beneath the Bodhi tree; a conspicuous convergence within the same medieval Tibetan literary environment (cf. the existing bon-tonpa-shenrab/buddhist-mara edge), not a claim of direct borrowing.
The temptation beneath the Bodhi tree is the founding scene of the tradition — the roster's Māra entry already presupposes this encounter with the man it tempts.
Śākyamuni himself is the nirmāṇakāya, the historical-body member of the Three Bodies doctrine — the doctrine and the man it names are inseparable.
Contemporary śramaṇa founders of the same Gangetic ferment (6th–5th c. BCE), each rejecting Vedic sacrifice and a creator-god for a path of personal awakening.
Maitreya is explicitly Śākyamuni's designated successor in the same lineage of Buddhahood, to descend when this teaching has been forgotten.
Yiguandao's Milefo IS Maitreya Buddha by name and prophecy, adopted directly into its own imminent-apocalypse theology.
Both are a world-renewing figure prophesied to appear at history's end and complete what the current dispensation could not — independently arrived at in Zoroastrian and Buddhist eschatology.
In the Pure Land triad Avalokiteśvara stands at Amitābha's side as chief attendant, compassion made concrete beside the Buddha of infinite light.
Nirvāṇa is the doctrinally explicit exit from the Wheel of Becoming — not a further turn of the cycle but its cessation.
Nirvāṇa is what remains once the no-self teaching is fully realized — the goal and the diagnosis that makes it thinkable.
Yama's mirror shows exactly what karma has accumulated — judgment here is exposure of the law, not a verdict imposed upon it.
Karma is explicitly named as the force turning the Wheel of Becoming, per the Wheel's own gloss.
Dependent origination is the doctrinal ground the Anātman entry rests on — no-self follows from the fact that everything, including the self, arises only conditionally.
The twelve links of dependent origination are literally painted around the rim of the Bhavacakra — doctrine and image are the same object.
Kṣitigarbha works the same hells Yama judges, in East Asian tradition sometimes standing alongside the Ten Kings as advocate for the damned.
His vowed domain is the hells stacked beneath Sumeru's own root, per the roster's cosmology.
The great bodhisattva pair of Mahāyāna devotion: Mañjuśrī's wisdom (prajñā) and Avalokiteśvara's compassion (karuṇā) as the two wings of the path.
Two-thirds of the Rigsum Gönpo, the Three Protectors of Tibetan Buddhism, alongside Avalokiteśvara.
Vajrapāṇi's thunderbolt is iconographically descended from Śakra-Indra's own vajra — the sky-king's weapon reissued to a wrathful bodhisattva protector.
Tārā is born, in the standard telling, from Avalokiteśvara's tears of compassion at the suffering he witnesses — mother and source within the same shard.
The stūpa enshrines the Buddha's own cremated relics — the cult object and the man are directly, physically continuous.
The stūpa's tiered dome and spire echo Sumeru's own stacked heavens — the cosmic mountain rebuilt at a scale a devotee can circumambulate.
The Buddha founded the monastic order at Sārnāth; the institution and its founder are directly attested in the Vinaya.
Mucalinda the nāga-king shelters the newly awakened Buddha from a week-long storm with his hood — the Dharma's chief serpent-cult hinge attached directly to its founder.
A second awakener-subdues-the-demon-host scene: Padmasambhava binds Tibet's hostile spirits to oath much as the Buddha sees through Māra's armies, in a later Vajrayāna register.
Delivered as the Buddha's first sermon at Sārnāth (Dhammacakkappavattana Sutta) — the founding teaching act of the entire dispensation.
the identical bodhisattva imported directly into Caodaism's pantheon under her own name and iconography.
The Jeseok gods take their very name from the Buddhist Śakra (Indra), a direct borrowing of a Buddhist deity-name into native shamanic birth-and-grain theology.
vast cyclical ages of arising and destruction, structurally convergent across unrelated cosmological traditions.
independent traditions both figuring reincarnation as a wheel to be escaped rather than a continuity to be embraced — no historical contact, structural analogy only.

BUDDHIST SEAM: under honji-suijaku, the Nachi shrine of the Kumano triad is specifically identified with Kannon (Avalokitesvara) — the same bodhisattva already folded into Zao Gongen's composite identity.

popular Chinese religion's Ten Kings of Hell tradition folds Yanluo Wang (Yama/Enma) in among the judges serving the courts of Mount Tai overseen by Dongyue Dadi — a documented Buddhist-Daoist syncretism of the underworld bureaucracy.
both stage the passage to ultimate reality through vast numbers of graded, impermanent intermediate forms rather than one instantaneous change.
the same bodhisattva, Guanyin, under a Yiguandao honorific title.

Mongolian Buddhism ran the identification the other direction from Erlik's regional descendants — Yama himself was rendered as Erlik Nomun Khan, fusing the shamanic spoiler-of-creation with the dharma-king of the dead under the Turkic name.

The Samguk Yusa, the myth's earliest source, writes Hwanin with the exact hanja for Śakra-devānām-Indra, the Buddhist king of the Trāyastriṃśa heaven — a documented scribal identification, not analogy: Korea's heavenly mandate-granter borrows the Buddhist Śakra's own name and rank.

dossier.essence: The tradition founded on the teaching (dharma) of Siddhartha Gautama, the Buddha, active in the Gangetic plain in roughly the 5th century BCE (his death is placed c. 483 or c. 400 BCE on the long and short chronologies). Now numbering on the order of half a billion adherents across Asia and worldwide, it centrally claims that conditioned existence is unsatisfactory (dukkha), driven by craving and ignorance, and that liberation (nirvana) is attainable by the eightfold path — without recourse to a creator god or an eternal self.
dossier.cosmos: Each world-system is built around Mount Sumeru, heavens stacked above and hells below, and arises, endures, and dissolves across immense kalpas — beginninglessly, by the impersonal law of dependent origination, with no creator behind the process. Beings migrate through six realms of rebirth — gods, titans, humans, animals, hungry ghosts, hell-beings — according to karma; even the gods, Indra included, are impermanent inmates of the wheel. At death rebirth follows karma until awakening ends the cycle; the doctrine of anatman denies any eternal self that transmigrates — only a conditioned stream continues.
dossier.practice: The institutional core is the sangha, the monastic order governed by vinaya discipline and supported by lay donors in a merit economy; lay practice rests on the five precepts, giving (dana), and observance days (uposatha). Cultivation combines ethical discipline, meditation (calm and insight), and study; devotional life centers on stupas, relics, images, and pilgrimage. Forms vary enormously across the Theravada, Mahayana, and Vajrayana worlds, from austere forest monasticism to elaborate liturgical and tantric systems.
dossier.canon: The Pali Tipitaka is the complete surviving early canon; the Mahayana world transmits vast additional sutra and commentarial corpora in the Chinese canon and the Tibetan Kangyur/Tengyur, with cosmology systematized in Abhidharma works, above all Vasubandhu's Abhidharmakosa (4th-5th c. CE).
dossier.reflects: It foregrounds order without a lawgiver — the religious impulse toward total causal law, with salvation as seeing through the self rather than appealing to a maker.
sources: Akira Sadakata, 'Buddhist Cosmology: Philosophy and Origins' (1997)
Vasubandhu, 'Abhidharmakosa' (4th-5th c. CE; trans. La Vallee Poussin / Pruden)
